channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2025 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2025 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Ultrafast dynamics of vibronically dressed core excitons in graphite: a femtosecond RIXS perspective
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 29 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41467-025-67919-7

Core excitons are strongly localised excitonic states impacting x-ray absorption and resonant inelastic scattering (RIXS) spectra. Here, the authors demonstrate an application of free electron laser-driven ultrafast RIXS spectroscopy to study previously unclear aspects of core exciton-phonon interactions in graphite.
item.dc:title: Ultrafast dynamics of vibronically dressed core excitons in graphite: a femtosecond RIXS perspective
item.dc:creator: Marco Malvestuto
Beatrice Volpato
Elena Babici
Richa Bhardwaj
Antonio Caretta
Simone Laterza
Fulvio Parmigiani

Peter Susnjar
Enrico Massimiliano Allaria
Alexander Darius Brynes
David Garzella
Luca Giannessi
Primož Rebernik
Filippo Sottocorona
Dino Novko

item.title: Superadiabatic topological pumping on photonic chips
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 29 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41467-025-67693-6

This work demonstrates superadiabatic topological pumping on photonic chips, achieving a 20-fold device miniaturization and high-efficiency operation across a 650–920 nm bandwidth, paving the way for ultracompact integrated photonic transports.
item.dc:title: Superadiabatic topological pumping on photonic chips
item.dc:creator: Jin-Lei Wu
Kai-Heng Xiao
Xiang Ni
Jin-Kang Guo
Xu-Lin Zhang
Qi-Dai Chen
Yan Wang
Ze-Zheng Li
Shi-Lei Su
Zhen-Nan Tian
Hong-Bo Sun

item.title: Printable polymer nanocomposites for scalable and architected radiative cooling
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41467-025-67831-0

Daytime radiative cooling materials typically require high filler loading or porosity. Here, authors introduce rheology–optics coupling to control particle dispersion, enabling printable low-filler PDMS–ZrO₂ composites for scalable and durable radiative coolers with versatile architectures.
item.dc:title: Printable polymer nanocomposites for scalable and architected radiative cooling
item.dc:creator: Kai Zhou
Songtao Tang
Pranto Karua
Fukang Wu
Sungmin Hong
Diya Patel
Gwendolyn M. Reeser
Donald M. Cropek
Paul V. Braun
Lili Cai
